title: The Best Movies at the Telluride Film Festival and the Triumphant Return of the Oscars Show | The Oscars Show
format: Sean gets back from his first Telluride and walks Amanda through the festival's best-picture hopefuls. They also sort through the Venice and Toronto lineups still to come and start working out the Best Actor race.

films.title: Ford v Ferrari
films.note: Opened the festival as the secret patron screening. A loud, old-fashioned true story from James Mangold, with Christian Bale getting the flashier of the two lead performances opposite Matt Damon.

films.title: The Two Popes
films.note: A surprise best-picture contender: Jonathan Pryce and Anthony Hopkins as Pope Francis and Pope Benedict, playing mostly as a buddy comedy despite the subject matter.

films.title: Uncut Gems
films.note: The Safdie brothers' loud, propulsive follow-up split the older Telluride crowd. Adam Sandler is fully committed as Howard Ratner, and Kevin Garnett turns in one of the great basketball-player performances.

films.title: Marriage Story
films.note: The consensus movie of the festival going in and out, built around an Adam Driver performance both hosts expect to be a major awards factor.

films.title: Waves
films.note: The movie Sean loved most at the festival, named alongside Marriage Story as one of the best things that played.

films.title: Judy
films.note: A safe fit for a Renée Zellweger transformation performance, and she's already visibly running her campaign. The film itself landed as more mixed.

films.title: Parasite
films.note: Bong Joon Ho's follow-up, ahead of its October release: as good as advertised, and fully itself rather than smoothed out for a best-picture run.

films.title: First Cow
films.note: Kelly Reichardt's quiet A24 drama about two men navigating a changing frontier society.

films.title: Diego Maradona
films.note: One of the documentaries in the lineup. Amanda caught it; Sean skipped docs at the festival in favor of narrative screenings.

films.title: Motherless Brooklyn
films.note: Edward Norton's long-gestating adaptation of Jonathan Lethem's novel. A rocky opening stretch gives way to a much stronger back half, though it strays far from the book's plot and period.

films.title: The Report
films.note: Adam Driver's second Telluride title, a Sundance holdover about Daniel Jones's years-long investigation into the CIA torture program.

films.title: The Assistant
films.note: A quiet dramatization of the Weinstein story told through the eyes of a studio assistant; it never names Harvey.

films.title: The Aeronauts
films.note: Felicity Jones and Eddie Redmayne in a hot-air-balloon survival picture; word out of the festival was mixed.

films.title: Pain and Glory
films.note: Pedro Almodóvar's film, one of the notable misses on Sean's schedule.

films.title: A Hidden Life
films.note: Terrence Malick's three-hour-plus WWII drama; the runtime alone made it hard to fit into a packed festival day.

films.title: The Climb
films.note: The one true word-of-mouth breakout, a cycling drama pitched as 'Sideways on bicycles,' from Sony Pictures Classics, and the one Sean regrets missing most.

films.title: Joker
films.note: The movie of Venice by conversation volume, though that doesn't necessarily translate to Oscar votes.

films.title: The King
films.note: Came out of Venice to weak reviews.

films.title: Wasp Network
films.note: Olivier Assayas's film also came out of Venice to weak reviews.

films.title: Ad Astra
films.note: James Gray's Brad Pitt vehicle got strong notices out of Venice. Sean calls it his most anticipated release of the year.

segments.heading: Stock Up Before Toronto
segments.note: unseen, but trending up ahead of TIFF

films.title: A Beautiful Day in the Neighborhood
films.note: Marielle Heller's Mr. Rogers film. Tom Hanks plays Rogers, but the lead is really Matthew Rhys as the journalist whose life he affects.

films.title: Bombshell
films.note: Jay Roach's Fox News drama, with Charlize Theron, Nicole Kidman, and Margot Robbie as the women who took on Roger Ailes.

films.title: Little Women
films.note: Mentioned in passing; neither host has seen it yet.

films.title: The Irishman
films.note: Premieres soon at the New York Film Festival, three and a half hours long, with Robert De Niro.

films.title: Cats
films.note: The trailer had already become a punchline around the festival.

films.title: Jojo Rabbit
films.note: Taika Waititi's 'anti-hate satire.' Early reactions are split between brilliant and not good.

films.title: Just Mercy
films.note: Michael B. Jordan and Brie Larson in a drama about Bryan Stevenson.

films.title: Lucy in the Sky
films.note: An astronaut drama with Natalie Portman and Jon Hamm.
Sam Mendes's WWI drama; not screening anywhere the hosts can get to yet.

films.title: Knives Out
films.note: Rian Johnson's family murder mystery, playing TIFF.

films.title: Once Upon a Time in Hollywood
films.note: The likely vehicle for both Leonardo DiCaprio and Brad Pitt: DiCaprio for lead, with Pitt widely expected to campaign in supporting despite the near-even screen time.

films.title: Dark Waters
films.note: Todd Haynes's Mark Ruffalo legal drama, still largely unseen and landing in a crowded late-November window.

films.title: Queen & Slim
films.note: Daniel Kaluuya's next, still to come out of Toronto.
